What's Actually Happening: Classifies intent using patterns learned during training. No external data. Routes to built-in agents first, then data-aware check, then registry, then factory.
Practical Impact in Production: Works well for known categories. Data-aware routing now catches entity-specific queries (SITE-, CELL-, compare) and forces to Investigator. May still misclassify novel query types not seen in training.
What Must Be Done for Production: 1. Add classification confidence score — if below threshold, ask user to clarify. 2. Log all misroutes for periodic analysis. 3. Let Agent Factory feed new categories back to Supervisor. 4. Data-aware routing already prevents Factory from handling data queries.

What's Actually Happening: Diagnoses from training memory (49K incident patterns). No live network data. No skills — single diagnose() function. Same diagnosis regardless of actual cell state.
Practical Impact in Production: Gives identical diagnosis for 'ERAB drop on SITE-001' and 'ERAB drop on SITE-002' — cannot tell that SITE-001 has interference while SITE-002 has a config issue. A real NOC engineer would check KPIs first. This agent doesn't.
What Must Be Done for Production: 1. Give tool access (same as Investigator) — auto-query KPIs + alarms for the reported cell before diagnosing. 2. Add skills: triage + diagnose + recommend (like Investigator but simpler). 3. Pass real data alongside user's symptom to the SLM. 4. Result: site-specific diagnosis instead of generic pattern matching.

What's Actually Happening: Generates YAML from learned config patterns. No skills. Not from real templates, not from current network state, not from vendor docs.
Practical Impact in Production: Generates plausible YAML but doesn't know current settings. May suggest antenna_tilt: 4 when cell already has it. Cannot produce vendor-specific CLI (Huawei MML, Nokia MOP). Parameter values may be wrong for FR2/mmWave.
What Must Be Done for Production: 1. Add config_check skill — query config endpoint before generating to get current baseline. 2. Generate config as a DIFF ('change X from current Y to Z') not a full template. 3. Index vendor documentation in RAG (Huawei Product Doc, Nokia MOP, Ericsson CPI). 4. Add FR2/mmWave-specific training data.

What's Actually Happening: With RAG: retrieves real 3GPP spec text → SLM synthesizes grounded answer. Without RAG (current default): SLM answers from memory, may fabricate spec references. No skills.
Practical Impact in Production: WITHOUT RAG: Says 'TS 38.300 Section 4.2' but section may not exist. Engineer trusts reference, looks it up, wastes time. WITH RAG: Cites real text from actual spec. Difference between useful and dangerous.
What Must Be Done for Production: 1. Enable RAG immediately (skip_rag=False in api_server.py). ChromaDB already built (64 GB, 3.5M vectors). Single highest-impact quick win. 2. Index vendor docs alongside 3GPP specs. 3. Add answer provenance — show which spec chunk was used. 4. Consider adding a 'verify' skill that cross-checks SLM answer against RAG.

What's Actually Happening: General telecom knowledge from training. No external data. No skills. Fallback for unmatched queries after data-aware routing and registry check.
Practical Impact in Production: Useful for general questions ('what is MIMO?') but gives textbook answers. Cannot answer 'what is our network's current MIMO configuration?' — no access to your data. Data-aware routing now prevents it from handling entity-specific queries.
What Must Be Done for Production: 1. Should be last resort — most queries route to specialist or Factory agent. 2. Add RAG access so answers are grounded in indexed documentation. 3. Long-term: as Factory creates more domain agents, GenericAgent usage should naturally decline.

What's Actually Happening: Only agent with skills AND external data. 5 skills: triage → diagnose → impact_assess → config_check → recommend. Each skill calls its own tools. Supports comparison mode (multi-site). 4-level guardrails prevent hallucination. Resource: ~60s for full investigation (6 SLM calls).
Practical Impact in Production: Behaves like a real engineer — checks data before diagnosing. Finds SITE-METRO-002 has interference because it looked at SINR. Skills chain provides structured investigation. HOWEVER: data is synthetic (Tier 1), skill chain selection is hardcoded keyword matching (Tier 2), ~60s high CPU per investigation.
What Must Be Done for Production: 1. Tier 2: Connect to O-RAN/ONAP Docker simulators for standards-compliant data. 2. Tier 3: Connect to real vendor NMS/OSS platforms. 3. Add topology lookup and alarm correlation as tools. 4. Move skill chain selection from hardcoded → learned from investigation logs (Tier 3). 5. Add structured function calling (Llama 3.1 native). 6. Consider GPU inference for production (5-10x faster). 7. Temperature 0.0-0.1 for deterministic output.

What's Actually Happening: Same model with specialized system prompt generated by Agent Factory. No skills, no tools, no RAG. Essentially GenericAgent with domain-specific framing. Data-aware routing prevents Factory from handling entity-specific queries — only pure knowledge domains reach Factory.
Practical Impact in Production: Creates 'SpectrumOptimizationAgent' on the fly for unseen domains. Answers from SLM training memory. Cannot fabricate network data because data-aware routing catches entity queries before Factory runs. Good for genuine knowledge domains (spectrum, billing, energy optimization).
What Must Be Done for Production: 1. Give Factory agents RAG access — when creating 'SpectrumPlanningAgent', auto-index relevant domain docs. 2. For promoted (active) agents: consider adding skills based on domain — spectrum agent could get a 'frequency_analysis' skill with spectrum analyzer tool. 3. Long-term: Skill Factory creates skills for Factory agents the same way Agent Factory creates agents. 4. Add disclaimer to Factory agent responses: 'This answer is from domain knowledge, not live network data.'
